specialties.name: Talent Acquisition Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: The Talent Acquisition Specialist (42T) role encompasses a broad range of responsibilities and skills, including developing and executing comprehensive recruitment plans, building relationships with candidates, conducting interviews and assessments, analyzing recruitment data, and applying leadership principles to drive Talent Acquisition operations. The 42T must possess expertise in communication, sales techniques, marketing (to include digital marketing), as well as the ability to interpret and apply data analysis and education principles. Key aspects of the role include aligning the Army brand to Talent Acquisition efforts, analyzing the labor market and competitor value propositions, and applying digital marketing strategies and tools. The 42T must also demonstrate proficiency in leadership, management, and negotiation, and stay current on best practices and trends in Talent Acquisition through ongoing training and development. The 42T Talent Acquisition Specialist supports change management in the organization by leveraging their expertise in recruiting, talent management, and marketing to attract, develop, and retain top talent, ultimately driving cultural transformation and facilitating the adoption of new initiatives and strategies. Overall, the 42T role requires a unique blend of strategic thinking, technical expertise, and interpersonal skills to attract, retain, and develop top talent and support the Army's talent management and workforce planning efforts. NOTE: this is a reclassification MOS for Soldiers already serving — it is not an option you can enlist directly into.

entry_requirements.other: Reclassification only — open to Soldiers already serving, selected by board/panel. Not available to new enlistees.

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 13-1071 (Human Resources Specialists), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $72,910
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: 42T is NOT an entry-level enlistment option: it is a reclassification MOS that serving Soldiers apply for through a MILPER-announced assessment and selection panel. You cannot ship to it from basic training. On the civilian side, Army recruiting experience maps well to corporate recruiting/HR, but no HR credential is conferred by the Army course. SHRM-CP and PHR both require a degree plus HR experience (or several years of HR experience without one); aPHR is the only one open with no experience and must be earned by exam.

specialties.name: Visual Information Equipment Operator-Maintainer
specialties.what_it_is: As a Visual Information Equipment Operator-Maintainer you will install, operate, and maintain visual information equipment and systems. This includes video teleconferencing systems, video switchers and audio mixers, synchronous generators, amplifying equipment, and photo imaging equipment. You’ll also work with closed circuit systems, visual imagery satellite, microwave, RF transmission, and cable distribution systems associated with VI operations that support Army, Joint, and Combined operations.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 27-4011 (Audio and Video Technicians), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $54,830
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: The Army trains you to run and fix broadcast/AV gear, but issues no civilian credential. Employers in commercial AV generally want the AVIXA CTS certification or an ETA electronics certification (CETa/BST); both are earned by exam and are not awarded with the MOS. Your Army experience counts toward eligibility, not toward the certificate itself.

specialties.name: Visual Information Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As a Visual Information Specialist, you’ll operate audio visual equipment and develop multimedia products that combine text, sound, graphics, animations, and video to support different areas of the Army such as Public Affairs, Military Intelligence, and Psychological Operations. You’ll create illustrations, map overlays, posters, graphs, charts, and web pages, while also performing unit level maintenance on assigned equipment.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

civilian_titles: Camera Operator, Television, Video and Film
relevant_certifications: Adobe Certified Professional in Digital Video Using Adobe Premiere Pro
Adobe Certified Professional in Visual Design Using Adobe Photoshop
civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 27-4032 (Film and Video Editors), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $70,980
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: This is a portfolio field, not a licensed one. There is no legal gap to close, but there is a practical one: civilian video and photo employers hire on a reel/portfolio and on named software proficiency (Premiere Pro, Photoshop, After Effects). Army work product is often restricted and cannot always be shown, so the real task before you separate is building a releasable portfolio and taking the Adobe Certified Professional exams, which the Army does not award.

specialties.name: Religious Affairs Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As a Religious Affairs Specialist, you’ll have the rewarding responsibility of supporting the unit ministry team, programs, and worship services, while also providing support to the Chaplains during missions and everyday activities. You’ll assist in planning and synchronizing religious support operations and coordinating the circulation of the unit ministry team to ensure the Soldiers’ spiritual readiness. You’ll also maintain reports, files, and administrative data for religious operations, and conduct specialized peer counseling for Soldiers in need of emotional support.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. Citizen; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 21-2021 (Directors, Religious Activities and Education), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $54,840
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: A 56M is NOT a chaplain and is NOT a counselor. Chaplains are commissioned officers who hold a graduate theological degree (typically an M.Div., about 72 semester hours) and an ecclesiastical endorsement; a 56M provides administrative, logistical and security support to that chaplain. The MOS does not confer any ministry credential or counseling license, and civilian pastoral or counseling roles require the degree and endorsement/licensure independently.

specialties.name: Biomedical Equipment Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As a Biomedical Equipment Specialist, you’ll maintain and repair medical equipment that Soldiers rely on, including X-ray systems and life-support monitors. You’ll diagnose problems, fix malfunctions, and install mechanical or radiological equipment to keep critical medical technology running.

entry_requirements.physical: Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting
entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

training_pipeline.location: Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 49-9062 (Medical Equipment Repairers), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $62,630
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: This is one of the cleanest crosswalks in the Army, but the credential is not automatic. Civilian hospitals hire on the AAMI CBET (Certified Biomedical Equipment Technician), which you must apply for and pass by exam; the 44-week AIT plus field experience satisfies the eligibility pathway, it does not grant the certificate. Note also that a 68A is not a biomedical engineer — that title, which goarmy lists as a related civilian job, requires an engineering degree.

specialties.name: Orthopedic Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As an Orthopedic Specialist, you'll assist with all kinds of patient care at an Orthopedic clinic—where patients are treated for pain and injuries to bones, joints, and muscles. You'll help prep patients for surgery, monitor their care afterwards, and even assist orthopedic surgeons with procedures in the operating room.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

training_pipeline.location: Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 29-2099 (Health Technologists and Technicians, All Other), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $48,790
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: A 68B is an orthopedic technologist/technician, NOT an orthotist or prosthetist. Orthotists and prosthetists (SOC 29-2091, the higher-paying title O*NET links to this MOS) require a master's degree and ABC certification — the Army gives you neither. The realistic civilian credential is the NBCOT Orthopaedic Technologist-Certified (OTC), earned by exam.

specialties.name: Operating Room Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As an Operating Room Specialist, you'll assist nurses in prepping patients for surgery and providing assistance to doctors during surgical procedures. You'll ensure that operating rooms and surgical tools are properly sterilized and inspected before and after procedures.

entry_requirements.physical: Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting
entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

training_pipeline.location: Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 29-2055 (Surgical Technologists), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $62,830
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: You are NOT a surgical nurse, despite goarmy listing 'Surgical Nurse' as a related civilian job — that is an RN, requiring a nursing degree and the NCLEX. A 68D maps to surgical technologist. The Army does not hand you the credential: you must pass the NBSTSA Certified Surgical Technologist (CST) exam, and a growing number of states now require CST certification by law to work in an OR.

specialties.name: Dental Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As a Dental Specialist, you'll receive the specialized training needed to assist Army dentists in the examination and treatment of servicemembers’ teeth. You'll help the dentist prepare for operations by selecting and arranging instruments and by taking patient’s dental impressions and X-rays. You’ll also manage the dental offices and patient records, perform preventive cleanings, take the patient’s blood pressure, pulse, and help administer anesthesia when needed.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

training_pipeline.location: Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 31-9091 (Dental Assistants), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $47,300
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: You are NOT a dental hygienist, even though goarmy lists it as a related civilian job. Hygienists require an accredited associate degree, the national board exam and a state license — the Army's 8-week AIT gives none of that. A 68E maps to dental ASSISTANT. To close the gap, sit the DANB Certified Dental Assistant (CDA) exam; many states additionally require state registration before you can perform expanded functions.

specialties.name: Physical Therapy Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As a Physical Therapy Specialist, you’ll help licensed Physical Therapists treat and rehabilitate patients suffering from a variety of injuries and illnesses in order to decrease their pain and promote a physically fit lifestyle so they can be in top physical condition. You’ll receive the intensive instruction needed to provide patient care and administer physical therapy treatments that will treat disabilities and prevent future injuries.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

training_pipeline.location: Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 31-2021 (Physical Therapist Assistants), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $65,510
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: This is the big one for 68F. You are NOT a physical therapist — that is a doctorate (DPT) plus state licensure. You are also not automatically a licensed Physical Therapist ASSISTANT: every state requires PTA licensure, which requires graduating a CAPTE-accredited PTA program and passing the NPTAE. Army AIT is not CAPTE-accredited. Without completing a civilian PTA associate degree you enter the civilian workforce as a physical therapist AIDE (SOC 31-2022), an unlicensed role that pays far less than the PTA figures below.

specialties.name: Patient Administration Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As a Patient Administration Specialist, you'll be entrusted with serving the military community and their families and providing essential support to the clinical staff of a hospital. You’ll perform administrative duties for patients, and you’ll utilize your knowledge of medical terms and statistical reports to manage medical records and keep track of patient health.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

training_pipeline.location: Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 29-2072 (Medical Records Specialists), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $50,250
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: The Army teaches you the workflow but awards no credential. The RHIT (Registered Health Information Technician) that employers ask for requires a degree from a CAHIIM-accredited program — 7 weeks of AIT does not qualify you to sit it. The reachable near-term credentials are the coding/billing exams (CBCS, CCA), which have no degree requirement, and those are what convert this MOS into a civilian job.

specialties.name: Optical Laboratory Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As an Optical Laboratory Specialist, you’ll read prescriptions and use advanced equipment to shape, coat, and assemble eyewear for Soldiers. You’ll maintain equipment and ensure troops have the precision eyewear they need to stay mission-ready.

entry_requirements.physical: Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting
entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

training_pipeline.location: Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX

relevant_certifications: American Board of Opticianry Basic Certification - National Opticianry Competency Examination (NOCE)
civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 51-9083 (Ophthalmic Laboratory Technicians), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $38,420
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Lab work (grinding and mounting lenses) is largely unlicensed in the civilian world, so the crosswalk to ophthalmic laboratory technician is direct. The gap appears if you want to move up to DISPENSING OPTICIAN — the customer-facing, better-paid role — which about half the states license, typically requiring the ABO National Opticianry Competency Examination (NOCE) and in some states an apprenticeship. The Army does not license you as an optician, and 68H is nothing like an optometrist.

specialties.name: Medical Logistics Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As a Medical Logistics Specialist, you’ll receive, store, and issue medical supplies and equipment while managing inventory records and quality assurance. You’ll ensure timely ordering and delivery of materials to support field training and deployments, supervise supply teams, and enforce compliance with safety procedures.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

training_pipeline.location: Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 43-5071 (Shipping, Receiving, and Inventory Clerks), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $43,190
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: 68J is a supply and logistics job that happens to sit inside a hospital — it is not clinical, and it does not qualify you for any patient-care role. There is no license to obtain, which means no legal barrier, but also no credential that distinguishes you. The AHRMM CMRP (Certified Materials and Resource Professional), the standard credential in healthcare supply chain, requires a degree plus experience or several years of experience on its own.

specialties.name: Medical Laboratory Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As a Medical Laboratory Specialist, you’ll perform lab tests and identify blood types to help diagnose and treat Soldiers. You’ll collect samples, run tests, manage blood products, and keep critical equipment running as it should.

entry_requirements.physical: Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting
entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

training_pipeline.location: Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 29-2010 (Clinical Laboratory Technologists and Technicians), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $61,890
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: To work in a civilian clinical lab you generally need MLT certification from ASCP or AMT, and a handful of states (California, New York, Florida and others) require a separate state license on top. The Army's 52-week AIT makes you eligible to sit the MLT(ASCP) exam through its military route, but the Army does not award the certification — you must apply and pass it. Note also that you are a technician, not a medical laboratory SCIENTIST (MLS), which requires a bachelor's degree.

special_pays.name: Enlistment bonus
special_pays.note: Bonus amounts change with Army needs and are set at contract signing; confirm the current figure with a recruiter.

specialties.name: Occupational Therapy Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As an Occupational Therapy Specialist, you’ll work with Registered Occupational Therapists to help Soldiers recover from injuries and illness through tailored treatment plans. You’ll teach recovery techniques and guide patients through physical and mental challenges caused by trauma.

entry_requirements.physical: Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting
entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

training_pipeline.location: Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX
Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 31-2011 (Occupational Therapy Assistants), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $68,340
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Same trap as 68F. Certified Occupational Therapy Assistant (COTA) requires graduation from an ACOTE-accredited program, the NBCOT exam, and a state license. The Army's 68L course is not ACOTE-accredited, so on separation you are qualified as an occupational therapy AIDE (SOC 31-2012), not an assistant, and the aide role pays substantially less. Closing the gap means completing a civilian OTA associate degree — the GI Bill will pay for it, but it is roughly two more years.

special_pays.name: Enlistment bonus
special_pays.note: Bonus amounts change with Army needs and are set at contract signing; confirm the current figure with a recruiter.

specialties.name: Nutrition Care Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As a Nutrition Care Specialist, you'll provide healthy and nutritious diets for patients and medical nutrition therapy for military service members, their families, retirees, and veterans. You’ll prepare and serve food that meets each patient’s individual medical condition for rapid recovery, disease management, and the promotion of health and wellness. You’ll work with a Registered Dietitian to create customized diets and specially balanced menus for patients, and you’ll teach wellness, nutrition, health, and good food choices.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

training_pipeline.stage: Advanced Individual Training (8 weeks, 92G, Culinary Specialist, 7 weeks, Nutrition Care Specialist)
training_pipeline.location: Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX

relevant_certifications: Certified Dietary Manager, Certified Food Protection Professional (CDM, CFPP)
civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 29-2051 (Dietetic Technicians), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $37,040
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: You are NOT a dietitian or a nutritionist, both of which goarmy lists as related civilian jobs. A Registered Dietitian Nutritionist now requires a master's degree, a supervised internship and a national exam. 68M maps to dietetic technician and food service management. The realistic and genuinely useful credential is the CDM/CFPP (Certified Dietary Manager), which the Army course prepares you for but does not award.

specialties.name: Radiology Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As a Radiology Specialist, you’ll operate X-ray and medical imaging equipment to diagnose and treat injuries and diseases. You’ll perform different types of imaging including digestive, respiratory, vascular, and prenatal scans, while ensuring patients receive quality care.

entry_requirements.physical: Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting
entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

training_pipeline.location: Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 29-2034 (Radiologic Technologists and Technicians), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $77,660
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Radiography is a licensed profession: most states require a state license, and essentially all employers require ARRT certification in Radiography (R). The good news is that the Army's 68P program is delivered through METC's accredited radiography program, so graduates are eligible to SIT the ARRT exam — but eligibility is not certification. You must pass the ARRT exam and then apply for licensure in the state where you intend to work; neither is automatic on discharge.

specialties.name: Pharmacy Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As a Pharmacy Specialist, you'll be an integral member of the healthcare team, preparing and issuing prescribed pharmaceuticals and medicines, while also maintaining pharmacy supplies and records under the supervision of pharmacists and doctors. You will evaluate orders, verify dosage calculations, and provide instructions to patients on how to properly take the medicine.

entry_requirements.physical: Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting
entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

training_pipeline.location: Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 29-2052 (Pharmacy Technicians), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $43,460
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: You are NOT a pharmacist — goarmy lists 'Pharmacist' as a related civilian job, but that is a PharmD doctorate plus state licensure. 68Q maps to pharmacy technician. Most states now require pharmacy techs to be registered with the state board of pharmacy and to hold the PTCB CPhT certification, which is earned by exam. The Army's 23-week AIT prepares you for it but does not confer it.

specialties.name: Veterinary Food Inspection Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As a Veterinary Food Inspection Specialist, you’ll oversee food safety, quality assurance, and protection from contamination of food consumed by Soldiers and their families. You’ll supervise food inspection and combined veterinary services, while using statistical sampling methods to ensure foods are inspected for freshness, proper storage and delivery, and packaged according to military standards.

entry_requirements.physical: Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting
entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

training_pipeline.location: Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 45-2011 (Agricultural Inspectors), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $50,990
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: 68R is food-safety inspection under veterinary supervision — you are not a veterinarian and not a vet tech. The catch on the civilian side is that the obvious destination jobs, federal food inspector posts with USDA/FSIS or FDA, are GS positions that typically require a bachelor's degree in a related science or a specific combination of coursework and experience; military inspection experience alone often does not meet the qualification standard. The NEHA CP-FS credential is the reachable one and is earned by exam.

specialties.name: Preventive Medicine Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As a Preventive Medicine Specialist, you'll be a major contributor when it comes to keeping the Army healthy and preventing the spread of infection and disease. You'll conduct laboratory tests to monitor the spread of bacteria, keep health records, and survey and track conditions for potential risks. You’ll have the skills and training necessary to recommend proper corrective actions to ensure good health for our Soldiers.

entry_requirements.physical: Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting
entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

training_pipeline.location: Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 19-5012 (Occupational Health and Safety Technicians), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $58,440
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Note which SOC the salary below is drawn from: TECHNICIAN (19-5012), not specialist. The Registered Environmental Health Specialist/Sanitarian (REHS/RS) that COOL lists as the flagship credential requires a bachelor's degree in environmental health or a related science before you may even sit the exam — 15 weeks of AIT does not substitute. Without a degree you enter as an occupational health and safety TECHNICIAN; the specialist roles (19-5011) that pay more are generally degree-gated.

specialties.name: Animal Care Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As an Animal Care Specialist, you'll provide medical care, management, and treatment for government-owned animals, including K-9 units, ceremonial horses, and the pets of Soldiers and their families. You will prevent and control the spread of diseases from animals to humans. You'll also help Army veterinarians conduct routine exams, administer medications, and perform surgical and emergency procedures for animals. It’ll be your responsibility to ensure the best possible treatment, so the animals can provide a great service to the Army.

entry_requirements.physical: Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting
entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

training_pipeline.location: Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 31-9096 (Veterinary Assistants and Laboratory Animal Caretakers), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $37,320
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: You are NOT a veterinary technician, despite goarmy listing that title first. Registered/licensed vet techs must graduate an AVMA-accredited program and pass the VTNE; the Army's 11-week 68T course is not AVMA-accredited, so in most states you are ineligible to sit the VTNE on military training alone. On separation you qualify as a veterinary ASSISTANT (SOC 31-9096), which pays far less than a credentialed vet tech. A few states offer alternate VTNE eligibility routes — check the specific state veterinary board before you count on it.

specialties.name: Respiratory Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As a Respiratory Specialist, your focus will be to help patients with chronic or sudden breathing problems. You'll be assisting doctors and advanced nurses in treating patients in both emergency and non-emergency situations, preparing blood samples for lab tests, and administering tests that check lung capacity and function.

entry_requirements.physical: Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting
entry_requirements.other: 6 months time in-service minimum required (this MOS is normally reached by reclassification, not direct enlistment); Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet qualifications to work in a medical setting; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

training_pipeline.location: Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 29-1126 (Respiratory Therapists), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $80,450
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Respiratory therapy is licensed in every state except Alaska, and licensure runs through the NBRC (CRT, then RRT). The Army's 68V program is delivered at METC through a CoARC-accredited course, which makes graduates eligible for the NBRC exams — but you still have to pass them and then obtain a state license before you can practice. Note also that goarmy lists no basic training in this MOS's pipeline and states a 6-month time-in-service minimum: 68V is generally reached by reclassification from another MOS, not straight off the street.

specialties.name: Behavioral Health Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As a Behavioral Health Specialist, you'll work under the supervision of psychologists, psychiatrists, and social workers to help administer mental health care to Soldiers. You'll conduct intake interviews and counsel patients to ensure they get the treatment they need to be productive and healthy members of the Army. Additionally, you'll help your fellow Soldiers learn to manage any existing mental health conditions, behavior problems, struggles with drugs and alcohol, and stresses that arise as part of Army service.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

training_pipeline.location: Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 29-2053 (Psychiatric Technicians), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $42,590
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: This is the most misunderstood MOS in the medical field. A 68X is NOT a counselor, therapist, psychologist or social worker — every one of those is a licensed profession requiring a master's or doctoral degree. 68X maps to psychiatric technician/aide. The credential you can actually reach is the RBT (Registered Behavior Technician): 40 hours of training plus a competency assessment and exam. If you want to become the clinician you were assisting, budget for a bachelor's plus a master's after service.

specialties.name: Eye Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As an Eye Specialist, you'll work with optometrists and ophthalmologists to conduct diagnostic vision tests and help care for and treat patients with eye or vision problems.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

training_pipeline.location: Medical Education and Training Campus (METC), JBSA-Fort Sam Houston, TX

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 29-2057 (Ophthalmic Medical Technicians), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $44,080
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: You are not an optometrist or an ophthalmologist — those are doctoral degrees. 68Y maps to ophthalmic medical technician, and here the crosswalk is genuinely good: IJCAHPO certification (COA, then COT) is earned by exam and military training plus documented work experience satisfies the eligibility route. The gap is small but real — the Army does not award the COT; you must apply and test for it.

specialties.name: Chemical, Biological, Radiological and Nuclear (CBRN) Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As a Chemical, Biological, Radiological, and Nuclear Specialist, you’ll protect the country against the threat of CBRN weapons of mass destruction, and you’ll decontaminate hazardous material spills or accidents. You’ll employ the most advanced equipment and coordinate defense systems against these weapons of mass destruction in support of joint and combined arms operations.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 19-4031 (Chemical Technicians), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $57,790
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: goarmy lists 'Bomb Disposal Unit Technician' and 'Firefighter' as related civilian jobs — a 74D is neither. Bomb disposal is 89D plus, on the civilian side, sworn law-enforcement status; firefighting requires a fire academy and state certification. What 74D genuinely maps to is hazmat and industrial safety work, and even there the standard entry ticket is the OSHA 40-hour HAZWOPER certification, which is a civilian course the Army does not give you. The CHMM that COOL lists requires a bachelor's degree plus four years of experience.

specialties.name: Recruiter
specialties.what_it_is: Recruits qualified personnel for entry into the Army in accordance with applicable regulations, supervises recruiting and recruiting support activities. NOTE: this is a reclassification MOS for Soldiers already serving — it is not an option you can enlist directly into.

entry_requirements.other: Reclassification only — open to Soldiers already serving, selected by board/panel. Not available to new enlistees.

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 13-1071 (Human Resources Specialists), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $72,910
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: 79R is NOT an entry-level enlistment option — it is a special-duty reclassification for serving NCOs, so a civilian cannot enlist into it. The Army is also actively transitioning this role into the new 42T MOS. On the civilian side, Army recruiting is real sales and staffing experience and transfers well, but no credential comes with it: SHRM-CP and PHR each require a degree plus HR experience, or several years of HR experience without a degree, and are earned by exam.

specialties.name: Career Counselor
specialties.what_it_is: Counsels and/or reenlist or extends qualified soldiers in the Army and supervises retention and manages attrition activities. NOTE: this is a reclassification MOS for Soldiers already serving — it is not an option you can enlist directly into.

entry_requirements.other: Reclassification only — open to Soldiers already serving, selected by board/panel. Not available to new enlistees.

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 13-1071 (Human Resources Specialists), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $72,910
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: 79S is NOT an entry-level enlistment option — it is a reclassification MOS for mid-grade NCOs (typically SGT(P)/SSG) already serving, so a civilian cannot enlist into it. Note the title is misleading on the civilian side: an Army 'Career Counselor' does retention and reenlistment administration, not licensed counseling. Civilian career counselor and school counselor roles generally require a master's degree and, in schools, state certification. The realistic civilian crosswalk is HR/employee relations, and SHRM-CP or PHR must be earned by exam.

specialties.name: Cargo Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As a Cargo Specialist, you'll be the cargo handling expert for everything the Army moves by land, sea, or air. You’ll ensure the Army receives supplies, weapons, the mail, and equipment on a daily basis all over the world. You will be trained in cargo documentation, the securing of cargo, and learn how to load and unload cargo manually or by operating material handling equipment like cranes and forklifts.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 53-1047 (First-Line Supervisors of Transportation and Material Moving Workers, Except Aircraft Cargo Handling Supervisors), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $61,890
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: The Army will certify you to operate its material-handling equipment, but that certification is internal — it does not transfer. Civilian employers require their own OSHA-compliant powered-industrial-truck (forklift) certification, which is employer-specific by law, and crane/rigging work needs NCCCO or equivalent. You also do not receive a Commercial Driver's License. None of this is a hard barrier; it is a set of short civilian courses you must budget for.

specialties.name: Watercraft Engineer
specialties.what_it_is: As a Watercraft Engineer, you’ll supervise or perform maintenance and repairs on Army watercraft and auxiliary equipment on marine vessels in order to keep them running at all times. You’ll perform daily systems checks, repair and maintain gasoline and diesel engines, troubleshoot and repair watercraft propulsion machinery, and repair and service lift machinery and engine-related electrical systems. To do this, you’ll train in basic electronics, hydraulics, and shop practices, as well as in engine operation and maintenance. You’ll also train in marine operations, the preventive maintenance of steering systems, and you’ll learn how to inspect and maintain the propellers and staffs.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 53-5031 (Ship Engineers), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $101,320
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: This is the highest-value and least-known transition in the batch. Civilian marine engineering jobs require a U.S. Coast Guard Merchant Mariner Credential (MMC) with an engineering endorsement, plus a TWIC card and STCW certificates. Army watercraft sea service CAN be credited toward the sea-time requirement for a USCG engineering license — but only if you document it correctly (sea service letters) while you are still in. Nobody does this for you, and the Army rating itself is not a USCG license. Start the paperwork before you separate or the sea time is very hard to reconstruct.

specialties.name: Transportation Management Coordinator
specialties.what_it_is: As a Transportation Management Coordinator, you'll plan and schedule transportation for troops, cargo, and equipment worldwide. You will document and inventory freight shipments, prepare cargo for travel, and determine the most efficient mode of transport and coordination for Soldiers and personnel to meet mission requirements.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 43-5011 (Cargo and Freight Agents), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $49,900
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: This is a paperwork-and-planning job and it moves to the civilian side with little friction — there is no license standing in your way. That is also the weakness: there is no credential proving what you know. The Army does not issue a Commercial Driver's License, and the logistics certifications that make a resume competitive (CLT, SCPro, CDGP for hazardous shipments) are all civilian exams you have to seek out yourself.

specialties.name: Railway Section Repairer
specialties.what_it_is: Maintains and repairs military railway track and roadbed (Reserve Component). NOTE: 88T is a Reserve-Component MOS and no longer appears in Army COOL's enlisted MOS list; its current status could not be confirmed against an official source.

entry_requirements.other: Reserve Component only. Current status of this MOS could not be confirmed against an official source.

civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: 88T is a Reserve-Component-only MOS and does not appear in the current Army COOL enlisted MOS list, which suggests it has been consolidated into 88U Railway Specialist — but I could not confirm its current status from an official source, so treat its existence and requirements as UNVERIFIED. Do not plan around this MOS without asking a recruiter to confirm it is still open. Track maintenance skills themselves transfer to civilian railroad maintenance-of-way work, where employers train and certify their own crews.

specialties.name: Railway Operations Crewmember
specialties.what_it_is: As a Railway Specialist, you’ll oversee the Army’s rail network capabilities and infrastructure assessments to assist with safe handling of important cargo. This includes rail planning and properly managing host nation or contracted rail assets, along with car inspections at route stops and following instructions received from signal towers or other trains.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

civilian_titles: Railroad Brake, Signal and Switch Operator
civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 53-4011 (Locomotive Engineers), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $77,400
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Here is the gap nobody mentions: civilian locomotive engineers must be certified under FRA regulation 49 CFR Part 240, and that certification is issued by the employing railroad, not by a school and not by the Army. You cannot arrive at a railroad already certified. Army rail experience makes you a strong hire into a conductor or trainee program, but you will still go through that railroad's own certification pipeline. 88U is also almost entirely a Reserve/National Guard MOS — active-duty rail jobs are very limited.

specialties.name: Ammunition Stock Control and Accounting Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As an Ammunition Stock Control and Accounting Specialist, you’ll perform stock control and accounting duties for ammunition, explosives, and associated explosive components using hardware and software systems and manual records. You’ll perform the issue, receipt, shipment, turn-in and maintenance transfer of ammunition.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 43-5071 (Shipping, Receiving, and Inventory Clerks), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $43,190
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: 89A is accounting and stock control for ammunition — it is a records job, not an explosives-handling job (that is 89B). The salary figures below are drawn from the inventory-clerk SOC for that reason. There is no license to earn and no legal barrier to civilian work, but also no automatic credential; supply-chain certifications (CLA/CLT, SCPro) are civilian exams. Do not expect the 'ammunition' in the title to open explosives-industry doors on its own.

specialties.name: Ammunition Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As an Ammunition Specialist, you’ll be responsible for the highly specialized care and management of ammunition, explosives, and their components. You’ll perform stock control and accounting duties, and you’ll be in charge of receiving, storing, issuing, and inspecting weapon systems to determine if they need repair or need to be destroyed. This means you’ll be in charge of blowing up expired or partially used explosives and other ordnance in controlled settings, a crucial part of keeping our troops safe.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 47-5032 (Explosives Workers, Ordnance Handling Experts, and Blasters), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $59,110
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Handling military ammunition does not license you to handle civilian explosives. Commercial blasters need a state blaster's license (requirements vary widely by state) and employers must hold ATF explosives licenses/permits; your Army qualification is not recognized by either. To haul explosives commercially you also need a CDL with a Hazardous Materials endorsement, including the TSA security threat assessment. The CHMM that COOL lists requires a bachelor's degree plus four years of experience.

specialties.name: Explosive Ordnance Disposal Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: As an Explosive Ordnance Disposal Specialist, you’ll be the Army’s preeminent tactical and technical explosives expert. You’ll have the advanced training and critical skills needed to disable and defeat explosive devices and weapons of mass destruction. You’ll research and identify military weapons, assist leadership in the preparation and use of advanced robotics, dispose of hazardous objects, and perform missions in support of Army units worldwide, across all environmental conditions.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

civilian_titles: Public Safety Bomb Technician (requires law enforcement status)
civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 47-5032 (Explosives Workers, Ordnance Handling Experts, and Blasters), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $59,110
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: The single most important thing to understand about EOD: military EOD does NOT make you a civilian bomb technician. Civilian public-safety bomb techs are sworn law enforcement officers first — you must be hired as a police officer or federal agent, and only then be sent to the FBI's Hazardous Devices School at Redstone Arsenal, which is the sole accreditation path in the United States. Your military EOD background makes you a very strong candidate for that pipeline, but it is not a substitute for it. The realistic direct-hire routes without a badge are defense contracting, UXO/munitions-response work (which typically wants OSHA HAZWOPER 40-hour), and hazmat/EHS roles.

special_pays.name: Enlistment bonus
special_pays.note: Bonus amounts change with Army needs and are set at contract signing; confirm the current figure with a recruiter.

specialties.name: M1 Abrams Tank System Maintainer
specialties.what_it_is: As an M1 Abrams Tank System Maintainer, you’ll perform maintenance on the suspension, steering, hydraulic, auxiliary power, fire suppression, and gas systems of Abrams tanks.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

relevant_certifications: Military Tactical Wheeled Vehicle (TWV) - Diesel Engines (MIL2)
Military Tactical Wheeled Vehicle (TWV) - Drive Train (MIL3)
Military Tactical Wheeled Vehicle (TWV) - Electrical/Electronic Systems (MIL6)
civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 49-3042 (Mobile Heavy Equipment Mechanics, Except Engines), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $63,980
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: The Abrams is a gas-turbine tank, so a large part of what you learn is powertrain-specific and has no civilian equivalent — this is the least transferable of the Army's mechanic MOSs. What does transfer is diagnostics, hydraulics, electrical systems and heavy-vehicle maintenance discipline. The Army awards no ASE certifications; the ASE Medium/Heavy Truck series (T-series) is the civilian standard and each test must be passed individually, with two years of hands-on experience required for full certification (military experience counts).

specialties.name: Utilities Equipment Repairer
specialties.what_it_is: As a Utilities Equipment Repairer, you’ll supervise and perform maintenance on vital utilities equipment and support systems, such as air conditioners and refrigeration units, to make sure they are kept in top working condition in any environment. You’ll train to read temperature and pressure gauges, make operating adjustments to utility equipment, test for leaks, perform preventative maintenance, troubleshoot malfunctions, and read and use technical manuals and diagnostic instruments.

entry_requirements.other: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident with a valid Green Card; 17 to 42 Years Old; Meet Tattoo Guidelines; No Major Law Violations; No Medical Concerns

civilian_titles: Heating, Air Conditioning and Refrigeration Mechanic
civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS National, SOC 49-9021 (Heating, Air Conditioning, and Refrigeration Mechanics and Installers), May 2024 — 10th–90th percentile annual wage; median $59,810
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: There is a hard legal gate here that the Army does not clear for you: under the Clean Air Act it is illegal to purchase or handle refrigerants without EPA Section 608 certification. No 608 card, no HVAC job — and the Army's 12-week AIT does not award it. It is a cheap, short civilian exam, but you must take it. Beyond that, many states and municipalities require an HVAC contractor or journeyman license (often several years of documented experience plus a state exam) before you can work unsupervised or pull permits. Note COOL rates this MOS's civilian O*NET matches as partial ('some duties'), not full.

unverified.reason: 42T is not published in goarmy.com's careers API (it is not an entry-level accession MOS). Line score 'GT 110 + ST 95' comes from secondary sources only; no official .mil page stating it was reachable (recruiting.army.mil and hrc.army.mil are WAF/JS-gated).
42T is not published in goarmy.com's careers API; no official training length/location was reachable.

goarmy.com publishes two conflicting ASVAB values for this MOS. Its 'minimum_asvab_line_score' field says 'EL 107' while the 'ASVAB' field that actually renders in the requirements list on the job page says 'EL 93 OR ST 91 + GT 97'. Recorded the 'minimum_asvab_line_score' value. Confirm with a recruiter before relying on it.

goarmy.com publishes a job page for 46V but states NO ASVAB line score on it (both the 'minimum_asvab_line_score' and 'ASVAB' fields are empty in its careers API). Value 'ST 91 / EL 93' is carried over from the earlier pass / secondary sources and could not be confirmed against an official source.

AIT location is INFERRED, not quoted. goarmy.com states the AIT length but never names the school. Army COOL attaches its 'METC Degree Partners' career note to this MOS, which ties its training to the Medical Education and Training Campus at JBSA-Fort Sam Houston; that linkage is the only basis for this location. Treat the school as likely-but-unconfirmed; the length is official.
